description: Personal essay — structuring a piece about moving cities

turns.content: I'm writing a personal essay about moving from a small town to a big city for college. I have lots of ideas but I don't know how to structure it.

Personal essays about transition are among the most written, which means the structure you choose matters a lot. The trap is chronological (I arrived, felt lost, found my people, grew). It is readable but predictable. A stronger approach is to build around a single concrete scene or image that contains the whole emotional truth of the transition. What is one specific moment from that move or early period that you keep coming back to?
There was this one moment in a crowded metro where I realized I didn't know a single person in the city. It hit me really hard.

That is your essay. Surrounded by people, completely alone — it is a perfect image for what a city move actually feels like. Structure around it:

Open in the metro: full sensory scene, end the opening on the realisation. Do not explain it yet.

Section 2: Pull back to the small town — one or two paragraphs about what 'knowing everyone' felt like, both the warmth and the limitation. This creates the contrast your metro scene needs.

Section 3: Two or three specific moments that show the texture of adjusting. Do not skip to 'and then I was fine.'

End: Return to the metro, or a later metro ride. Same setting, different relationship to it. End on complexity, not resolution.

This structure works because the metro becomes a motif, not just a scene.
That's really helpful. How long should a personal essay like this be?

For a college application or literary magazine context, 650-800 words is the sweet spot. For a personal blog, 1200-1500 words gives you room to develop the small town contrast. The biggest mistake in personal essays is overwriting the emotion — the reader should feel it from the concrete details, not from adjectives. 'I felt lonely' is weaker than 'I counted the stops on the metro map because I had nothing else to look at.' Show the state through action and sensory detail, and trust the reader to feel what you felt.
